transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Ian. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I’ve been feeling a bit off lately. I had this plan for the weekend to get some sewing done and catch up on work, but it didn’t really go as I hoped. I ended up just... watching TV instead. It’s frustrating, you know? I thought I could handle it, but it just felt harder than it should have.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you had some expectations for your weekend that didn’t quite pan out, and that’s left you feeling frustrated. It’s understandable to feel that way when things don’t go as planned. Can you tell me more about what made it feel harder than you expected?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I mean, I thought I knew what I was doing. I’ve sewn before, and I’ve managed my work tasks, so it shouldn’t have been that difficult. But when I sat down to actually do it, I just couldn’t focus. I kept thinking, “Why can’t I do this?” It felt like I was just stuck, and that made me really annoyed with myself. I guess I just expected it to be easier.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like there was a disconnect between your expectations and what you experienced. You’ve done sewing and managed your work before, so it makes sense that you would feel frustrated when you couldn’t focus. That feeling of being stuck can be really challenging. When you were thinking, “Why can’t I do this?” what emotions were coming up for you in that moment?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I felt a mix of anger and disappointment, I guess. I was mad at myself for not being able to do something I usually can handle. It’s like I was letting myself down. There was also this guilt, like I should have been able to push through it. It just made me feel... irritated, and then I ended up avoiding it all by just zoning out in front of the TV. It’s like I couldn’t face the frustration anymore.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you experienced a lot of intense emotions—anger, disappointment, and guilt—when you felt you weren’t meeting your own expectations. It’s understandable that you would want to escape those feelings by zoning out in front of the TV. Avoidance can sometimes feel like the easier option when things get overwhelming. How do you usually cope when you feel that mix of frustration and guilt?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I tend to just... distract myself. Like, when I feel that frustration building up, it’s easier to just turn on the TV or scroll through my phone. I don’t really know if that’s a good way to cope, but it’s what I do. I just want to avoid feeling that anger or disappointment. Sometimes I think I should be doing something more productive, but it’s hard to push myself when I’m feeling like that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like distraction has become a go-to coping mechanism for you when those difficult emotions arise. It makes sense that you would want to avoid feeling anger or disappointment, especially when they can be so uncomfortable. You mentioned thinking you should be doing something more productive—what do you think holds you back from pushing yourself in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s a mix of things. Part of me feels like I should just be able to get it done, you know? But when I sit down and can’t focus, it makes me doubt myself. I start thinking, “What’s wrong with me? Why can’t I just do this?” And then that just adds more pressure, which makes it even harder to get started. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle of frustration and self-doubt, and I don’t really know how to break out of it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a cycle at play where your expectations and self-doubt create additional pressure, making it even harder to focus and get started. That feeling of being stuck can be really challenging, especially when you’re caught between wanting to accomplish something and feeling like you can’t. When you think about that self-doubt and frustration, what do you wish you could tell yourself in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I wish I could remind myself that it’s okay to struggle sometimes. That it doesn’t mean I’m failing or that I’m not capable. Maybe I could tell myself that everyone has off days and that it’s normal to not be productive all the time. I think if I could be a bit kinder to myself in those moments, it might help ease some of that pressure. But it’s hard to actually do that when I’m feeling overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s really insightful that you recognize the importance of self-compassion and understanding that struggling is a part of being human. It sounds like you have a desire to be kinder to yourself, especially during those overwhelming moments. Acknowledging that everyone has off days is a powerful perspective. What do you think might help you remember to be kinder to yourself when you’re in the midst of that frustration?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe having some sort of reminder could help—like a note or something I could see when I’m feeling stuck. Just a simple phrase that says it’s okay to take a break or that it’s normal to have tough days. I’ve also thought about trying to take a few deep breaths or step away for a moment to clear my head before diving back in. It’s just hard to remember to do those things when I’m caught up in the frustration.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like really practical ideas—having a reminder and taking a moment to breathe can be helpful tools to ground yourself when frustration arises. It’s understandable that it can be challenging to remember those strategies in the heat of the moment. What do you think might help you integrate those reminders into your routine so that they’re more accessible when you need them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could set a reminder on my phone or put a sticky note on my desk where I usually work. Something visual that I can’t miss. I could also try to make it a habit to check in with myself regularly, like taking a minute to pause and breathe before I start a task. If I can make it part of my routine, it might be easier to remember when I’m feeling overwhelmed. It’s just about finding a way to make those reminders stick, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are great ideas! Setting a phone reminder or using a sticky note can create that visual cue to help you remember to check in with yourself. Making it part of your routine by taking a minute to pause and breathe sounds like a gentle way to ease into your tasks. It seems like you’re really thinking about how to create a supportive environment for yourself. How do you feel about taking these steps moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit hopeful about it, actually. It’s nice to think that I could have some tools to help me manage those frustrating moments better. I know it won’t be perfect, but having a plan feels like a step in the right direction. I guess I just need to remind myself to be patient with the process, too. It’s a bit daunting, but I think I can give it a try.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling hopeful and that you see these steps as a positive direction. Acknowledging that it won’t be perfect and allowing yourself patience in the process is an important mindset. It sounds like you’re ready to give this a try, and that’s a significant step. As you move forward, what do you think will be the most important thing to keep in mind to help you stay on track?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the most important thing will be to remember that it’s okay to have setbacks and that progress isn’t always linear. I need to be gentle with myself and not get too caught up in feeling like I’m failing if things don’t go perfectly. Just taking it one day at a time and being open to adjusting my approach if something isn’t working might help. I guess it’s about finding that balance between trying to be productive and being kind to myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a really thoughtful perspective, Ian. Recognizing that progress isn’t always linear and allowing for setbacks is key to maintaining a healthy mindset. It sounds like you’re aiming for a balance between productivity and self-compassion, which is so important. As you embark on this journey, how do you think you’ll remind yourself to stay focused on that balance when challenges arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could create a little mantra or phrase that I can repeat to myself when I start feeling overwhelmed. Something like, “It’s okay to take a break” or “I’m doing my best.” I could also try to reflect on my progress regularly, maybe at the end of each week, to remind myself of the small wins, even if they seem minor. Keeping a journal might help with that, too. Just having those reminders could help me stay grounded when challenges come up.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are excellent ideas! Creating a mantra to repeat during overwhelming moments can serve as a powerful reminder to be kind to yourself. Reflecting on your progress and acknowledging those small wins at the end of each week can help reinforce that positive mindset. Keeping a journal sounds like a great way to track your thoughts and feelings, too. It seems like you’re building a solid toolkit for yourself. How do you feel about incorporating these practices into your routine?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel pretty good about it, actually. It’s nice to have some concrete strategies to work with. I think having a mantra and reflecting on my progress could really help me stay focused on that balance I’m trying to achieve. The journal idea feels like a safe space to express my thoughts, too. I just hope I can remember to stick with it, but I guess that’s part of the process. Overall, I’m feeling more optimistic about taking these steps.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m glad to hear you’re feeling optimistic and that you have some concrete strategies to work with! It sounds like you’re setting yourself up for success with your mantra, reflections, and journaling. Remembering to stick with it can be challenging, but being patient with yourself as you navigate this process is key. It’s great that you’re creating a safe space for your thoughts. As you move forward, what do you think will help you stay committed to these new practices?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting specific times for these practices might help me stay committed. Like, maybe I could set aside a few minutes each day to repeat my mantra or write in my journal. Having a routine could make it feel more natural. I could also remind myself of why I want to do this in the first place—like to feel less overwhelmed and more in control. If I can keep that in mind, it might motivate me to stick with it, even on the tougher days.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan! Setting specific times for your practices can definitely help make them feel more integrated into your daily routine. Reminding yourself of your motivations—like wanting to feel less overwhelmed and more in control—can serve as a powerful anchor on those tougher days. It’s clear you’re putting a lot of thought into how to make this work for you. How do you feel about the journey ahead, knowing you have these strategies and motivations in place?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of hope and a bit of nervousness, to be honest. It’s encouraging to have these strategies and motivations, but I also know that change can be challenging. I’m hopeful that having a plan will make it easier to navigate those tough moments. I guess I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to take it one step at a time and that it’s a process. Overall, I’m feeling more prepared to face whatever comes my way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely natural to feel a mix of hope and nervousness when embarking on a journey of change. Acknowledging that it’s a process and allowing yourself to take it one step at a time is a wise approach. It sounds like you’re building a strong foundation for yourself, and being prepared to face challenges with your strategies in mind is a positive mindset. As you move forward, how do you think you’ll celebrate your progress, no matter how small, along the way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could celebrate my progress by treating myself to something small when I reach certain milestones, like finishing a week of journaling or consistently using my mantra. Maybe I could enjoy a favorite snack or take some time to do something I love, like sewing or watching a movie I’ve been wanting to see. I think acknowledging those small wins will help keep me motivated and remind me that I’m making progress, even if it feels slow at times.
